title: CODE OF PRACTICE FOR THE PREVENTION OR REDUCTION OF CIGUATERA POISONING CXC 83-2024

sections.section: CODE OF PRACTICE FOR THE PREVENTION OR REDUCTION OF CIGUATERA POISONING CXC 83-2024
sections.summary: Document title: Code of Practice for the Prevention or Reduction of Ciguatera Poisoning (CXC 83-2024).

Describes ciguatoxins (CTXs) as lipophilic toxins produced mainly by Gambierdiscus (and some Fukuyoa) that bioaccumulate in herbivorous and predatory fish and shellfish, resist cooking/freezing, comprise many analogues, and are expanding geographically; ciguatera poisoning (CP) causes acute gastrointestinal, neurological and cardiovascular symptoms, is globally underreported, and analytical detection and validated reference materials are currently limited, prompting FAO/WHO guidance.

Heading introducing recommended practices for risk management of CTXs, covering surveillance/monitoring, governmental measures, analytical methods, data sharing, consumer and healthcare advice, and ecosystem impacts.

Recommends competent authorities establish or strengthen surveillance of CTXs in algae, sentinel species and fish for consumption using a two-tier approach (biological screening then chemical confirmation), determine regional causative organisms, consider sentinel species and mapping, maintain epidemiological databases, use local fisher knowledge, and conduct case investigations (identify species, capture area/date, amount consumed, and recover meal remnants) to inform area restrictions.

Advises that, where data permit, authorities may consider maximum levels for CTXs but acknowledges analytical limitations, and lists possible regional measures (e.g. banned or advisory species lists, conditional imports, size limits, positive lists, first-sale checks, HACCP requirements and inspections), plus reporting, advisories, and expert consultation.

Calls for standardized, validated testing protocols for seafood, algae and passive samples to ensure comparability, recommends molecular species identification (e.g. DNA barcoding), quantitative methods or summed-toxin reporting with appropriate reference materials, storage of contaminated sample aliquots for reference material development, collaboration on methods, and a two-tier testing strategy (functional biological screening followed by confirmatory chemical methods such as LC‑MS when structures/reference materials permit), while noting the CoP does not prescribe specific methods.

Encourages competent authorities to share guidance and best practices, provide training for scientists, consult CP experts and international agencies (e.g. IAEA, IOC‑UNESCO), and share database approaches and annual monitoring summaries to aid other regions.

sections.section: 4.6 Advice to Consumers and Healthcare Professionals
sections.summary: Requires competent authorities to provide consumer and healthcare advice on CP (e.g. fact sheets, recreational advisories, educational materials) describing characteristic symptoms and timing, advising avoidance of implicated fish parts and restricted areas, preservation of meal remnants for testing, seeking medical care, precautions on breastfeeding/sexual transmission, and clinician training/notification procedures.

sections.section: 4.7 Minimizing negative impacts of human activity
sections.summary: Advises authorities to assess whether human activities are contributing to increases in Gambierdiscus/Fukuyoa or CTX‑contaminated fish and to consider steps to mitigate such ecosystem impacts.
Annex I heading (non-substantive): introduces an annex.

sections.section: List of marine organisms known or suspected to be associated with CP
sections.summary: Provides an excerpted, non‑exhaustive tabular list (scientific/common names and locations) of marine organisms known or suspected to be associated with ciguatera poisoning, sourced from the FAO/WHO expert report.
